comments:
This manuscript is a broad survey of convolutional neural networks (CNNs), covering basic components (convolution, pooling, activations), many architectural advances (NIN, Inception, VGG, etc.), loss functions, regularization and optimization techniques (Dropout, BatchNorm, SGD variants), fast computation approaches (FFT, low-rank factorization, quantization), and a wide set of applications (classification, tracking, pose estimation, text spotting, saliency, action recognition, scene labeling). The text is well organized into logical sections and provides many references to the key works in each subarea. The survey would be valuable for readers seeking an organized entry point into CNN literature circa the time it was written.

Strengths:
- Comprehensive scope: the paper touches on most major directions of CNN development and a broad selection of representative methods and applications.
- Structured presentation: grouping by components (layer design, pooling, activations, losses, regularization, optimization, fast methods) makes the review easy to navigate.
- Practical focus: inclusion of implementation/practical considerations (initialization, BatchNorm, FFT, GPU libraries) is useful for practitioners.

Weaknesses / areas for improvement:
- Limited critical synthesis: the manuscript primarily catalogs methods rather than providing deeper comparative analysis. There are few quantitative comparisons, no summary tables comparing trade-offs (accuracy, complexity, memory), and minimal discussion of when to prefer one approach over another.
- Lack of theoretical discussion: the paper acknowledges limited theoretical understanding of CNNs but does not deeply engage with theoretical attempts or open problems (reasons for depth benefits, generalization theory, interpretability, robustness). A dedicated subsection on open theoretical challenges and future directions would strengthen the contribution.
- Presentation issues in the provided text: there are duplicated and repeated fragments at the end (likely an artifact of extraction) that should be cleaned in the final version.
- Currency and depth: as a survey, depth in any single subtopic is necessarily limited. Some readers may expect more detailed algorithmic descriptions, illustrative comparisons, or a consolidated table of recommended practices.

Recommendations to authors:
- Add summary tables that compare methods on key axes (performance, computation, parameter count, typical use cases) to help readers quickly understand trade-offs.
- Include a clear section on open problems and promising future directions (theoretical understanding, unsupervised/weakly supervised learning, robustness, hardware-aware design, etc.).
- Clean formatting and remove repeated text fragments present in the current document.
- Where possible, provide brief, concrete guidelines or a decision flow (e.g., when to use spectral pooling vs. spatial pooling, when to favor low-rank factorization) to increase practical utility.

Overall, this is a solid, useful survey that compiles a large body of work into a readable structure. With improved synthesis (comparative tables, clearer recommendations, and cleanup of formatting issues) it would be an even more valuable reference for researchers and practitioners.
